narrative:
It’s a cold and wintry St. Patrick’s Day around the High Country and Southwest Virginia! Snow showers and flurries will become more scattered in nature today, before finally coming to an end by this evening. Temperatures will be very cold, running around 25-degrees below normal for afternoon highs. Other than another passing snow flurry very early on Wednesday, skies will be mostly cloudy. It will be cold, just not as cold as Tuesday. Moderation kicks into gear afterwards.

See our Winter Weather Event page for our snow spotter reports and the snow accumulation forecast map.

This week's Monday Message is now available to our subscribers.

The "Mix and Match" Special Offer continues on all the Snowman O'Mometer Pint Glass and Mug (or you can long for a 5 Golfball Day with that pint glass as well). Buy one, and get another drinkware item at 40% off.

Monday-Tuesday

White On Your Green

Last Updated Tue March 17 at 10:30AM by Scott Stephens

It’s a cold and wintry St. Patrick’s Day around the High Country and Southwest Virginia! Snow showers and flurries will become more scattered in nature today, before finally coming to an end by this evening. Temperatures will be very cold, running around 25-degrees below normal for afternoon highs. Other than another passing snow flurry very early on Wednesday, skies will be mostly cloudy. Forecast Discussion

The snow shower machine will tend to be the energizer bunny today, becoming more scattered in nature, but probably not concluding until this evening. Then, another upper level disturbance brushes the region early Wednesday with perhaps a few additional flurries. It will be unseasonably cold.

Moderation will kick into gear after midweek, sending temperatures back closer to where they should be in mid-March.

The pattern is dry, other than a weak warm front producing a few light showers Thursday evening.

For Friday and through next weekend, we are unseasonably mild again with a mix of clouds and sunshine during this period. Sunday looks like our warmest day.

Then, a back door cold front settles southward early next week, reducing temperatures and also providing our next opportunity for light rainfall. A cool wedge may also begin to set up shop by that time.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.

Monday Message for March 16, 2026

Quiet weather returns for western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia after a hyper-active Monday around the region. Severe weather is followed by a sharp cold shot and mountain snow shower episode, then we moderate nicely for late week through the weekend.

Mar 16, 2026
Mid-March Madness

A strong cold front crosses the Southern Appalachians Monday morning and moves into the Foothills Monday afternoon.

Mar 16, 2026
Severe Weather Season Around The Corner

As our March weather has become more changeable and volatile, it's a reminder that severe weather season is almost upon us.

Last week was Severe Weather Awareness Week in North Carolina; This week is Virginia's turn.

While western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia may have less overall severe weather than our surrounding neighbors, we still have our fair share, and it's a great time to review your severe weather emergency/preparedeness procedures ahead of time.
